content:
# Luke Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 108-109 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_016_page_001.jpg, LUK_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Jesus also said to his disciples, "There was a rich man who had a manager, and this manager was reported to him as squandering his possessions. **2** So he called him in and said, 'What is this I hear about you? Turn in a complete account of your management, because you can no longer be my manager.' **3** The manager said to himself, 'What am I going to do? My master is taking away my position. I am not strong enough to dig ditches, and I am too ashamed to beg. **4** I know what I will do, so that when I am removed from my position, people will welcome me into their homes.'

**5** "So he called in each one of his master's debtors. He asked the first, 'How much do you owe my master?' **6** He replied, 'A hundred measures of olive oil.' The manager told him, 'Take your bill, sit down quickly, and write fifty.' **7** Then he asked another, 'And how much do you owe?' He replied, 'A hundred measures of wheat.' The manager told him, 'Take your bill and write eighty.'

**8** "And the master commended the dishonest manager for acting so shrewdly. For the people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the people of the light. **9** And I tell you, use worldly wealth to make friends for yourselves, so that when it runs out, they will welcome you into eternal dwellings.

**10** "Whoever is faithful with very little is also faithful with much, and whoever is dishonest with very little will also be dishonest with much. **11** So if you have not been faithful with worldly wealth, who will trust you with true riches? **12** And if you have not been faithful with what belongs to someone else, who will give you what is your own?

**13** "No servant can serve two masters. Either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and money."

**14** The Pharisees, who loved money, heard all of this and were sneering at Jesus. **15** He said to them, "You are the ones who justify yourselves in the eyes of others, but God knows your hearts. What people value highly is detestable in God's sight.

**16** "The Law and the Prophets were in effect until John. Since then, the good news of the kingdom of God is being proclaimed, and everyone is pressing into it forcefully. **17** But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for a single stroke of a letter of the Law to drop out.

**18** "Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, and the man who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.

**19** "There was a rich man who dressed in purple and fine linen and lived in luxury every day. **20** And a poor man named Lazarus was laid at his gate, covered with sores, **21** longing to eat what fell from the rich man's table. Even the dogs came and licked his sores. **22** In time the poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham's side. The rich man also died and was buried.

**23** "In Hades, where he was in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham far away, with Lazarus at his side. **24** So he called out, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, because I am in agony in this flame.' **25** But Abraham replied, 'Child, remember that in your lifetime you received your good things, while Lazarus received bad things. But now he is comforted here and you are in agony. **26** And besides all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those who want to cross over from here to you cannot, nor can anyone cross over from there to us.'

**27** "He answered, 'Then I beg you, father, send Lazarus to my father's house, **28** for I have five brothers. Let him warn them, so that they will not also come to this place of torment.' **29** Abraham replied, 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them listen to them.' **30** He said, 'No, father Abraham! But if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent.' **31** Abraham said to him, 'If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.'"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **16:1-2 — "was reported to him as squandering his possessions"**: The word DIASKORPIZŌN (diaskorpizōn, "squandering" or "scattering") is the same verb used to describe the prodigal son's behavior in 15:13. Luke has placed these two parables back to back deliberately. The prodigal squandered his own inheritance; the manager squanders someone else's property. The connection invites the reader to ask: what do we do with resources that were never ours to begin with? The manager's crisis — imminent termination — forces a reckoning that the prodigal only reached through famine and pig-feeding. Both stories are ultimately about what happens when the bill comes due.

- **16:3 — "I am not strong enough to dig ditches, and I am too ashamed to beg"**: The manager's self-assessment is brutally honest. SKAPTEIN (skaptein, "to dig") refers to hard manual labor — breaking ground, digging irrigation trenches. EPAITEIN (epaitein, "to beg") was the last resort of the destitute. The manager has lived as a white-collar professional; he knows he cannot survive in either the physical labor market or the shame economy of begging. His predicament is real, and his solution, while ethically questionable, demonstrates the kind of urgent creativity Jesus wants to see applied to matters of eternal significance.

- **16:5-7 — The debt reductions**: The first debtor owes BATOUS HEKATON ("a hundred baths") of olive oil — roughly 800-900 gallons, the yield of about 150 olive trees, worth approximately 1,000 denarii (nearly three years' wages for a laborer). The manager cuts it in half. The second debtor owes KOROUS HEKATON ("a hundred cors") of wheat — roughly 1,000 bushels, the yield of about 100 acres, worth approximately 2,500 denarii. The manager reduces it by twenty percent. The differing discounts may reflect the different profit margins on each commodity, or the manager may be removing his own commission from each bill. Either way, the debtors are now personally indebted to the manager — they will feel obligated to help him when he loses his position. The manager is converting his master's wealth into social capital for himself.

- **16:8 — "the master commended the dishonest manager"**: This is one of the most startling verses in the Gospels. The HO KYRIOS ("the master" or "the lord") here almost certainly refers to the master within the parable, not to Jesus himself — though the ambiguity may be intentional. The commendation is not for the dishonesty but for the PHRONIMŌS (phronimōs, "shrewdly" or "prudently") — the manager's strategic thinking in a crisis. The broader principle follows: HOI HUIOI TOU AIŌNOS TOUTOU ("the sons of this age" — people oriented toward worldly affairs) are PHRONIMŌTEROI ("more shrewd") than HOI HUIOI TOU PHŌTOS ("the sons of the light" — God's people) when it comes to dealing with their own generation. Jesus is not praising fraud; he is lamenting that those who claim to follow God often show less urgency, creativity, and strategic thinking about eternal matters than scoundrels show about temporary ones.

- **16:9 — "use worldly wealth to make friends for yourselves"**: The phrase MAMŌNA TĒS ADIKIAS ("mammon of unrighteousness") does not necessarily mean money obtained dishonestly. ADIKIA here functions more broadly — it is "unrighteous mammon" in the sense that all worldly wealth belongs to a system tainted by injustice and impermanence. Jesus' counsel is to deploy this inherently compromised resource in the service of relationships and generosity, so that when it EKLIPĒ ("fails" or "runs out" — as all wealth eventually does), the result will be welcome into AIŌNIOUS SKĒNAS ("eternal dwellings" or "everlasting tents"). The image deliberately contrasts the temporary nature of money with the permanence of what it can purchase when used generously. This is Jesus' economic theology in miniature: wealth is a tool, not a treasure, and its only lasting value lies in what it does for others.

- **16:10-12 — Faithfulness with little and much**: These three parallel sayings form a tight logical chain. Verse 10 establishes the principle: character revealed in small matters predicts character in large ones. Verse 11 applies it: if you have been unfaithful with ADIKŌ MAMŌNA ("unrighteous mammon" — worldly money), who will entrust you with TO ALĒTHINON ("the true" — genuine, eternal riches)? Verse 12 sharpens it further: if you have not been faithful with TO ALLOTRION ("what belongs to another" — since all material wealth ultimately belongs to God), who will give you TO HYMETERON ("what is your own" — the Sinaiticus reading, versus TO HĒMETERON "what is ours" in some manuscripts)? The progression moves from general principle to specific application to personal challenge. All wealth is a stewardship test for something greater.

- **16:13 — "You cannot serve both God and money"**: MAMŌNAS (mamōnas) is an Aramaic word meaning "wealth" or "property," here personified as a rival deity. The verb DOULEUEIN (douleuein, "to serve as a slave") implies total, exclusive allegiance — a slave belongs wholly to one master. Jesus does not say it is difficult to serve both; he says it is IMPOSSIBLE (OU DYNASTHE, "you are not able"). The two allegiances are structurally incompatible. Money demands accumulation; God demands generosity. Money promises security; God demands trust. Money measures worth by possession; God measures it by self-giving. The choice is binary.

- **16:14 — "The Pharisees, who loved money, were sneering at Jesus"**: Luke's editorial comment is devastating. The word PHILARGYROI (philargyroi) means literally "silver-lovers" — those whose affections are directed toward money. They EXEMYKTĒRIZON (exemyktērizon) Jesus — a word meaning "to turn up one's nose at," "to sneer," or literally "to snort through the nostrils." Their contempt for Jesus' teaching about money reveals precisely the condition Jesus just diagnosed: they are serving mammon while claiming to serve God. Their sneering is not a counterargument; it is a symptom.

- **16:15 — "What people value highly is detestable in God's sight"**: The word BDELYGMA (bdelygma, "abomination" or "detestable thing") is one of the strongest terms of revulsion in biblical Greek — the same word used for idols and for the desecrating horror prophesied in Daniel. Jesus is saying that the entire value system the Pharisees have constructed — a system in which wealth signals divine favor and social prestige equals spiritual standing — is not merely mistaken but ABOMINABLE to God. This is a total inversion of their worldview. What they parade before others as evidence of God's blessing, God regards with the same revulsion he directs at idolatry.

- **16:16-17 — The Law, the Prophets, and the Kingdom**: These two verses hold together a tension that runs throughout Luke's Gospel. Verse 16 announces a new era: the Law and the Prophets governed until John the Baptist; since then, the kingdom of God is EUANGELIZETAI ("being proclaimed as good news") and everyone BIAZETAI ("presses into it forcefully" or "is being forced into it" — the verb is ambiguous between middle and passive). But verse 17 immediately prevents any conclusion that the new era abolishes the old: it would be easier for heaven and earth to vanish than for one KERAIAN (keraian, "serif" or "hook" — the smallest decorative stroke on a Hebrew letter) to fall from the Law. Jesus is not abolishing Torah; he is fulfilling it with an intensity the Pharisees have not imagined. The kingdom does not lower the bar — it raises it.

- **16:18 — Divorce and remarriage**: This verse appears abruptly, but it is not a non sequitur. It functions as a concrete illustration of verse 17: the Law's demands are not relaxed in the kingdom era; they are intensified. The Pharisees had developed elaborate legal mechanisms for divorce (permitted under Deuteronomy 24:1-4), effectively using the letter of the Law to evade its spirit. Jesus' pronouncement — that remarriage after divorce constitutes MOICHEUEI (moicheuei, "adultery") — restores the radical permanence of the marriage covenant as God originally intended it. In context, this is also connected to the money theme: the Pharisees justify both financial accumulation and easy divorce by manipulating Scripture, but the kingdom demands a deeper fidelity in both domains.

- **16:19-21 — The rich man and Lazarus**: This is the only parable in which a character is given a proper name — LAZAROS (Lazaros, from Hebrew Eleazar, meaning "God helps"). The rich man is unnamed, which is a deliberate reversal: in the ancient world, the rich were famous and the poor were anonymous. In Jesus' telling, it is the poor man whose name is known in heaven. The rich man wears PORPHYRAN (porphyran, "purple") — the most expensive dye in the ancient world, derived from sea snails, associated with royalty — and BYSSON (bysson, "fine linen") — expensive Egyptian fabric worn next to the skin. He feasts LAMPROS ("splendidly" or "brilliantly") every day — not occasionally but as a daily lifestyle. Lazarus, by contrast, is EBEBLĒTO ("had been thrown" or "laid" — the passive voice suggests he cannot move himself) at the rich man's gate, covered with HELKŌN (helkōn, "ulcers" or "open sores"). He desires to eat APO TŌN PIPTONTŌN ("from what falls") from the rich man's table — not scraps deliberately given but crumbs that accidentally drop. The dogs that lick his sores are not pets providing comfort but unclean street animals adding indignity to his suffering. The two men are separated by nothing more than a gate — and by everything that a gate represents.

- **16:22-23 — Death as the great reversal**: Both men die, but their deaths are described asymmetrically. Lazarus is carried by angels to TON KOLPON ABRAAM ("the bosom of Abraham" — the place of honor at Abraham's banquet table, the ultimate expression of covenant belonging). The rich man simply dies and is buried — ETAPHĒ (etaphē), likely with an elaborate funeral that contrasts bitterly with what follows. In HADĒS (Hadēs — the realm of the dead, not the later Christian concept of hell as a permanent state but the intermediate state of the departed), the rich man is EN BASANOIS ("in torments"). The reversal is total. The gate that separated them in life has become a CHASMA MEGA ("great chasm") in death, but the positions are now inverted.

- **16:24-26 — "Between us and you a great chasm has been fixed"**: The rich man calls Abraham PATER ("Father"), claiming covenant relationship — the very thing the Pharisees relied upon (see 3:8, "We have Abraham as our father"). Abraham responds with TEKNON ("Child") — the same tender term the father used for the older brother in 15:31. Abraham does not deny the relationship; he simply explains that it cannot override the consequences of a lifetime of indifference. The word MNĒSTHĒTI ("remember") is the key: the rich man is asked to recall that he received TA AGATHA SOU ("your good things") during his life while Lazarus received TA KAKA ("the bad things"). The possessive pronoun is telling — the rich man claimed the good things as his own rather than recognizing them as a stewardship from God. The CHASMA MEGA ("great chasm") that is ESTĒRIKTAI ("has been fixed" or "established permanently") between them is not arbitrary divine punishment — it is the eternal solidification of the distance the rich man himself maintained during his lifetime. He kept Lazarus at his gate; now the distance has become uncrossable.

- **16:27-31 — Moses, the Prophets, and resurrection**: The rich man's request shifts from self-concern to concern for his five brothers — he wants Lazarus sent as a messenger from the dead to warn them. Abraham's response is striking: they have MŌUSEA KAI TOUS PROPHĒTAS ("Moses and the Prophets") — that is, the Scriptures. The implication is that Scripture, properly read, already contains everything necessary to know about God's concern for the poor and the danger of wealth-hoarding. The rich man's protest — "if someone goes to them from the dead (EK NEKRŌN), they will repent" — receives the most devastating reply in the parable: "If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead (EK NEKRŌN ANASTĒ)." The phrase EK NEKRŌN ANASTĒ ("rises from the dead") is unmistakably loaded with resurrection language. Luke's audience, reading this after Easter, would hear the bitter irony: someone DID rise from the dead, and many still did not believe. The parable's final word is a sober warning: no miraculous sign can substitute for a heart willing to hear what God has already said. Scripture is sufficient. The problem is never the evidence; it is the will.

- **16:19-31 — The parable as a whole**: This parable brings together every theme of Luke 16. The dishonest manager used wealth shrewdly to secure his future (vv. 1-9); the rich man used wealth exclusively on himself and secured nothing. Jesus taught that faithfulness with worldly wealth is a test of readiness for true riches (vv. 10-12); the rich man failed the test catastrophically. Jesus declared that one cannot serve both God and money (v. 13); the rich man served money, and the Pharisees who sneered at this teaching (v. 14) are implicitly identified with him. The parable of the rich man and Lazarus is not primarily about the afterlife — it is about what money reveals about the human heart, and what the prophetic tradition has always demanded: justice, mercy, and solidarity with the suffering poor. The chasm in Hades merely makes visible the chasm that already existed at the gate.
